demerit_desc_1: Installed hot and cold waterlines are not horizontally aligned and were placed without using a faucet sample or reference.
demerit_desc_2: Non-filling of checklist form for waterproofing works
demerit_desc_3: Wall primer paint is not properly applied, particularly along the lower edges of the walls and columns.
demerit_desc_4: Scaling of primer painted wall surface at 2nd floor
demerit_desc_5: Water closet stub-out does not meet  The standard 12" requirement from the wall
demerit_desc_6: Ceiling openings for exhaust from T&B and kitchen are not provided (rework)
summary_opm:
Operation Evaluation Comment - Engr. Jeffered Alican
(Project Manager - Based on Technical and Managerial Evaluation)

The evaluation reflects that he is still in the process of developing his capabilities as a Project Manager. Based on the overall assessment covering both technical and managerial competencies, he obtained a score of 49.04%, which is slightly below the expected threshold for the Project Manager role.
This score is derived from observations and site inspections conducted over the past three months. The following areas were identified as needing improvement during the execution of the project under his supervision:
1. Limited comprehension of the approved plans.
2. Inconsistent enforcement of plans during actual
execution.
3. Work activities were not carried out in accordance with the established sequence.
4. Monitoring of manpower utilization relative to daily accomplishments was insufficient.
5. Look‑ahead schedules and weekly/monthly targets were not prepared, making progress tracking difficult.
6. Risk anticipation and proactive assessment.

7. Technical guidance and supervision for the engineering team were insufficient, affecting overall coordination and performance.

8. Comprehensive management control was not achieved due to gaps in strategic foresight and planning.

On the other hand Engr. Jeffered Alican showed maturity and improvement on the following aspects:

1. His leadership has been consistently present and supportive.

2. Documentation and procedural systems have been established and are currently being applied

3. He is regarded positively by his team and maintains strong working relationships.

4. He shows flexibility and can quickly adapt to evolving circumstances on site.

Overall, the evaluation highlights that while Engr. Jeffered Alican is still progressing in his development as a Project Manager, he has shown meaningful strengths that support his growth in the role. The identified areas for improvement provide clear opportunities for him to enhance his technical oversight, planning, and strategic management capabilities. At the same time, his steady leadership presence, positive team relationships, adaptability, and commitment to implementing documentation systems demonstrate a solid foundation on which further development can be built. With focused guidance and continued effort, he has the potential to significantly strengthen his performance and meet the full expectations of the position
summary_qaqc:
📄 QAQC Evaluation Comment – Engr. Jeffered Alican (Project Manager – Based on QAQC Section Score: 43%)
The QAQC evaluation for Engr. Jeffered Alican reflects a developing alignment with the quality governance expectations for a Project Manager. Based on evaluation file, his QAQC section registered an average of 43%, which is below the Project Manager threshold of 2.6–3.8, and falls closer to the lower competency bands.

This score is consistent with the document’s QAQC summary, which shows “Section Average of – 43.00% . It is important to emphasize that this evaluation system is newly implemented, and as expected, most personnel—including Engr. Alican—are registering lower baseline scores.

The scoring matrix is intentionally more stringent, focusing on measurable compliance, documentation accuracy, and NC‑related performance. Thus, the current rating should be interpreted as a starting benchmark, not a reflection of diminished capability.
His QAQC performance was also directly affected by the Non‑Conformance (NC) records logged on his site. The uploaded file shows multiple NC entries under the Demerits Log, including misalignment of waterlines, incomplete waterproofing checklists, and grout quality issues, all marked as “Accountable – 2” . These NCs fall under his scope of responsibility as Project Manager and therefore lowered his QAQC monthly averages.
Despite these challenges, Engr. Alican demonstrated consistent coordination with QAQC, involvement in inspections, and adherence to ITP‑based workflows across the months covered by this evaluation.

Recommendations for Improvement

  • Strengthen preventive QAQC controls to reduce recurring NCs.

  • Conduct more frequent joint inspections with QAQC to catch deviations early.

  • Improve documentation completeness (ITPs, checklists, NCRs, CAPAs).

  • Reinforce subcontractor compliance through clearer instructions and follow‑through.

  • Implement construction meeting with weekly NC review meetings to track closure and prevent recurrence.
summary_qs:
The QS evaluation shows a moderate performance level, with a section score of 50.50%, as reflected in the uploaded file under “Section Average (5%) – 50.50%” . While this score is higher than the QAQC rating, it still falls below the expected Project Manager threshold of 2.6–3.8.

The new scoring system again plays a major role in the lower rating. The evaluation criteria for QS emphasize cost discipline, expenditure tracking, and alignment of financial decisions with project objectives—areas where the new system applies stricter, more quantifiable standards.

Additionally, QS performance was indirectly affected by the NCs recorded on site, as quality deviations often lead to rework, material wastage, and schedule impacts—all of which influence QS scoring. The presence of multiple NCs in the Demerits Log contributed to lower efficiency indicators and reduced the overall QS average.

Across the months covered, Engr. Alican maintained steady coordination with the QS team, ensured documentation flow, and supported cost‑related decision‑making. These efforts provide a strong foundation for improvement in the next cycle.

Recommendations for Improvement

  • Strengthen cost‑tracking discipline and ensure all expenditures are supported by complete documentation.

  • Improve coordination with QS for timely submission of quantity validations and cost‑related reports.

  • Reduce rework‑related costs by addressing root causes of NCs.

  • Enhance planning accuracy to minimize material wastage and avoid unplanned procurement.

  • Conduct monthly cost‑performance reviews to align QS outputs with project targets.

Engr. Jeffered Alican is now taking on a more active role in leading the QS function. As he transitions into this responsibility, it is important that the key recommendations identified in this evaluation are consistently observed and developed by both him and his team. Strengthening the full QS skill set—including contract understanding, cost estimating, proposal preparation, sound judgment, and client relationship management—will be essential in ensuring that the QS unit operates as a complete and reliable support group for project delivery.

His leadership is expected to guide the team toward improved accuracy, stronger cost control, and better coordination with site operations. By enhancing these competencies and applying them consistently, Engr. Alican and his team will be better positioned to meet the performance expectations required under the Project Manager role.
